class_schedule.topic: Systems of Linear Equations and Gaussian Elimination
learning_objectives: Represent systems of equations as augmented matrices
Apply Gaussian elimination to solve systems
Identify consistent, inconsistent, and underdetermined systems
readings: Strang, Introduction to Linear Algebra, Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: Linear algebra is the most applied branch of mathematics in computer science. Machine learning, computer graphics, data science, cryptography, and quantum computing all depend on vectors, matrices, and linear transformations. This course builds the mathematical foundation for all of them.
key_concepts: Systems of linear equations and their geometric interpretation
Augmented matrix representation
Row operations: swap, scale, add multiple
Gaussian elimination: forward elimination and back substitution
Row echelon form and reduced row echelon form
Solution types: unique, infinite, no solution
lecture_notes.demonstration: Solve a 4x4 system using Gaussian elimination, interpret each equation as a hyperplane, and show how the solution is the intersection point
setup_requirements: Calculator or NumPy for matrix operations

review.value: An upper triangular form where each leading entry (pivot) is to the right of the pivot above it, and all entries below each pivot are zero; this form enables back substitution to find the solution

Gaussian elimination on an n x n matrix requires approximately (2/3)n^3 operations; this cubic complexity makes it practical for moderate-sized systems but motivates iterative methods for very large systems

learning_objectives: Perform matrix multiplication and understand its geometric meaning

lecture_notes.introduction: Matrices are not just arrays of numbers; they represent linear transformations. Multiplying a vector by a matrix transforms it: rotating, scaling, reflecting, or projecting. Every 3D graphics pipeline, every neural network layer, and every PCA reduction is a matrix multiplication.
key_concepts: Matrix-vector multiplication: Ax = b as a linear combination of columns
Matrix-matrix multiplication: composition of transformations
Identity matrix and inverse: the do-nothing and undo transformations
Geometric interpretation: rotation, scaling, reflection, projection
Matrix transpose and its properties
lecture_notes.demonstration: Visualize 2D linear transformations: show how rotation, scaling, and shearing matrices transform a grid of points, then compose two transformations by multiplying their matrices

review.value: Ax applies the transformation represented by A to vector x; the columns of A show where the standard basis vectors go. This geometric view makes matrix operations intuitive rather than mechanical

Matrix multiplication is not commutative: AB is generally not equal to BA. In graphics, this means the order of transformations matters: rotate then translate gives a different result than translate then rotate

learning_objectives: Determine whether a set of vectors forms a basis for a vector space
Compute the rank and nullity of a matrix
Understand the relationship between column space, null space, and solutions to Ax=b

lecture_notes.introduction: Vector spaces provide the abstract framework for linear algebra. Understanding basis, dimension, and subspaces reveals the structure hidden in data: the rank of a matrix tells you how much independent information it contains, and the null space tells you about redundancy.
key_concepts: Vector space axioms: closure under addition and scalar multiplication
Subspaces: column space, null space, row space
Linear independence: no vector is a combination of the others
Basis: a linearly independent spanning set
Dimension: the number of vectors in any basis
Rank-nullity theorem: rank(A) + nullity(A) = number of columns
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compute the column space, null space, rank, and nullity of a 4x5 matrix, then use the rank to determine how many independent features exist in a dataset

review.value: The rank of a data matrix tells you the number of truly independent features; if a 100-feature dataset has rank 10, there are only 10 independent dimensions of variation. PCA finds these dimensions
The null space of A contains all vectors x such that Ax = 0; if the null space is non-trivial, the system Ax = b has infinitely many solutions (if any), differing by null space vectors

learning_objectives: Compute eigenvalues and eigenvectors of a matrix
Understand the geometric meaning of eigendecomposition
Apply diagonalization to simplify matrix computations

lecture_notes.introduction: Eigenvectors are the directions along which a linear transformation acts by simple scaling. Eigenvalues tell you the scaling factor. This decomposition is the key to understanding PCA, Google's PageRank, quantum mechanics, and vibration analysis.
key_concepts: Eigenvalue equation: Av = lambda * v
Characteristic polynomial: det(A - lambda*I) = 0
Computing eigenvalues from the characteristic polynomial
Computing eigenvectors for each eigenvalue
Diagonalization: A = PDP^(-1) where D is diagonal
Geometric interpretation: eigenvectors define the natural axes of the transformation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compute eigenvalues and eigenvectors of a 3x3 covariance matrix, visualize the eigenvectors as the principal axes of an ellipsoid, and connect to PCA

review.value: PCA computes the eigenvectors of the data covariance matrix; the eigenvector with the largest eigenvalue points in the direction of maximum variance. Projecting onto the top k eigenvectors reduces dimensionality while preserving the most information

Google's PageRank is the dominant eigenvector of the web's link matrix; a page's rank is proportional to the probability that a random surfer lands on it, which is determined by the structure of links pointing to it

learning_objectives: Compute the SVD of a matrix and interpret its components
Apply SVD to low-rank approximation and data compression
Understand the connection between SVD, PCA, and least squares

lecture_notes.introduction: The singular value decomposition is arguably the most important matrix factorization in applied mathematics. It reveals the rank, the best low-rank approximation, and the geometric structure of any matrix. SVD powers recommendation systems, image compression, and natural language processing.
key_concepts: SVD: A = U * Sigma * V^T where U and V are orthogonal and Sigma is diagonal
Singular values: the diagonal entries of Sigma, measuring importance
Low-rank approximation: keeping only the top k singular values
SVD for image compression: storing k components instead of the full image
Connection to PCA: SVD of centered data gives the principal components
Pseudoinverse and least squares: solving overdetermined systems
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compress an image using SVD at different rank approximations (k=1, 5, 20, 50), visualize the reconstruction quality vs compression ratio, and show the singular value spectrum

review.value: The best rank-k approximation of a matrix (minimizing the Frobenius norm of the error) is obtained by keeping only the top k singular values and their corresponding singular vectors; SVD gives the optimal compression for any matrix

Collaborative filtering decomposes the user-item rating matrix using SVD; the latent factors (singular vectors) capture hidden preferences, and missing ratings are predicted by reconstructing from the low-rank approximation

questions.question: Given A = [[2,1],[4,3]], compute A^(-1) and verify that A * A^(-1) = I.
Describe geometrically what the matrix [[0,-1],[1,0]] does to vectors in R^2. What is the transformation and by what angle?
questions.answer: This matrix rotates vectors 90 degrees counterclockwise. We can verify: applying it to [1,0] gives [0,1] (the x-axis maps to the y-axis), and applying it to [0,1] gives [-1,0] (the y-axis maps to the negative x-axis). This is consistent with a 90-degree counterclockwise rotation. The general rotation matrix for angle theta is [[cos(theta), -sin(theta)],[sin(theta), cos(theta)]]; with theta = 90 degrees (pi/2), cos=0 and sin=1, giving [[0,-1],[1,0]].

rubric.key: Verification using basis vectors or rotation formula

questions.question: Find the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of A = [[4,2],[1,3]].
questions.answer:
Characteristic polynomial: det(A - lambda*I) = (4-lambda)(3-lambda) - 2*1 = lambda^2 - 7*lambda + 10 = (lambda-5)(lambda-2) = 0.

Eigenvalues: lambda_1 = 5, lambda_2 = 2.

For lambda_1 = 5: (A-5I)v = 0 gives [[-1,2],[1,-2]]v = 0. Row reduce: [-1,2|0]. So -v1 + 2v2 = 0, giving v1 = 2v2. Eigenvector: [2,1] (or any scalar multiple).

For lambda_2 = 2: (A-2I)v = 0 gives [[2,2],[1,1]]v = 0. Row reduce: [1,1|0]. So v1 + v2 = 0, giving v1 = -v2. Eigenvector: [-1,1] (or any scalar multiple).

rubric.key: Shows work for null space computation

questions.question: Explain why PCA uses the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix. What do the eigenvalues represent in the context of data analysis?
questions.answer: The covariance matrix captures how features vary together. Its eigenvectors point in the directions of maximum variance in the data (principal components), and the eigenvalues give the amount of variance along each direction. PCA projects data onto the eigenvectors with the largest eigenvalues, which captures the most information (variance) in the fewest dimensions. Eigenvalues ranked from largest to smallest tell you how much of the total variance each principal component explains, enabling you to choose how many components to keep for a desired fraction of explained variance.

rubric.key: Eigenvectors as directions of maximum variance

major_assignments.title: Linear Algebra Applications in Computer Science
major_assignments.description: Apply linear algebra to three CS problems: (1) implement PCA from scratch using eigendecomposition for dimensionality reduction, (2) build an image compression system using SVD, and (3) implement a simple recommendation system using matrix factorization.
objectives: Implement PCA using eigendecomposition of the covariance matrix
Build an image compressor using truncated SVD
Implement collaborative filtering using matrix factorization
Visualize and analyze results for all three applications
deliverables: Python implementations of all three applications
Analysis report (3-4 pages) with results, visualizations, and discussion
Comparison of compression quality vs dimensionality for PCA and SVD

criteria: Covariance matrix computed correctly
Eigendecomposition produces correct principal components
Dimensionality reduction preserves specified variance percentage
Visualization shows data before and after projection
SVD decomposition implemented or used correctly
Multiple rank approximations compared
Compression ratio and quality metrics computed
Visual comparison of original vs compressed images
Matrix factorization produces reasonable predictions
Training and evaluation split properly
RMSE or similar metric computed on held-out data
Comparison with a baseline method
Mathematical derivations are correct and complete
Code is clean and reproducible
Report connects theory to practical results
Visualizations effectively communicate findings
Correct PCA implementation with variance analysis
Effective SVD image compression with quality metrics
Working recommendation system with evaluation
Clear documentation connecting linear algebra to applications

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of linear algebra concepts

homework_assignments.description: Weekly problem sets with computations and proofs

major_assignments.description: Application project connecting linear algebra to CS

midterm_examination.description: Written exam covering systems, matrices, and vector spaces

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam covering all linear algebra topics

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all lectures. More than 3 unexcused absences may reduce participation grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late homework loses 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of approaches is encouraged. All written solutions must be your own work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted work must be your own. Copying from solution manuals or other students is plagiarism.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need a calculator and Python with NumPy and matplotlib for the application project.
